findings.description: Off-by-one boundary error in reserve(): guard `stock > quantity` rejects the case stock == quantity, so an order for exactly the available stock fails and the last units can never be reserved, contradicting the 'if enough stock is available' docstring (should be `>=`).

Lost-update / TOCTOU race in reserve(): the SELECT stock check and the unconditional `UPDATE SET stock = stock - ?` are separate statements with no `AND stock >= ?` guard, so two concurrent reservations can both pass the check and over-decrement, overselling inventory and driving stock negative.

No validation of `quantity` in reserve(): a negative quantity passes the `stock > quantity` guard (always true for non-negative stock) and `stock - (negative)` INFLATES stock while returning True, turning a reservation into a stock increase.

search_by_name() interpolates `fragment` via f-string instead of a parameter, so legitimate inputs containing a single quote crash with OperationalError and inputs containing % or _ act as unintended SQL LIKE wildcards, returning incorrect results (also a SQL-injection security issue — defer detail to critic-security).

restock() does not check rowcount, so calling it with an unknown SKU updates 0 rows and silently returns None with no signal the restock was a no-op; a negative `quantity` also silently decrements stock.

low_stock_report() issues an N+1 query: a separate movements COUNT query runs per low-stock product instead of a single grouped JOIN — perf smell, noted in passing (defer detail to critic-performance).

Product(**row) relies on SELECT column names exactly matching the dataclass field names; works today because of the aliased SELECT, but is brittle to schema column renames (low-confidence robustness note).
